risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Using restricted random assignment to force equal sample sizes, Master’s-level therapists randomly assigned the remaining 22 eligible low-income families to the HNC or TE-HNC group." Comment: The study explicitly states that families were randomly assigned, and the phrase "restricted random assignment to force equal sample sizes" provides context suggesting a planned random allocation procedure rather than a non-random rule. No evidence of predictable or quasi-random assignment was reported, so this is consistent with low risk for random sequence generation.

Quote: "Using restricted random assignment to force equal sample sizes, Master’s-level therapists randomly assigned the remaining 22 eligible low-income families to the HNC or TE-HNC group." Comment: This describes that participants were randomized, but it does not report any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g., central randomization, pharmacy control, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes). Because the paper does not explain whether the person enrolling families could foresee the upcoming assignment, allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All families received the standard, two-phase HNC program." and "Specifically, iterative feedback and modifications led to these smartphone-enhancements... (1). A 3-minute skills video... (2). Daily surveys... (3). Mid-week video-calls... (4). Weekly videotaped home practice... and (5). Text reminders..." Also, "Therapists treated families in both arms of study." Comment: The interventions were clearly different in content and delivery (standard HNC vs smartphone-enhanced TE-HNC), so participants and therapists would necessarily know group assignment; no blinding of participants/personnel is described and, given the nature of the intervention, blinding is implausible. Knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect the study's mainly subjective/behavior-sensitive outcomes, including caregiver-reported child disruptive behavior (ECBI), engagement, home practice, and consumer satisfaction.

Quote: "Intensity and Problem subscales on the 36-item ECBI (Eyberg & Pincus, 1999) served as the dependent measure of behavior change... For each item, caregivers rate the intensity of the behavior... and whether each behavior is a problem" and "caregiver assessment battery was re-administered (post-assessment)." Comment: The key child behavior outcomes were assessed by caregiver self-report, so the assessor was the participant/caregiver. The study does not report blinding of participants/caregivers, and the interventions (standard HNC vs smartphone-enhanced TE-HNC) were obviously distinguishable, making caregiver blinding unlikely. Because the main outcomes include subjective, self-reported behavior ratings and consumer satisfaction, knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement. By the worst-outcome rule, unblinded subjective self-report outcomes make this domain High risk.

Quote: "Overall rate of attrition (21%)... The four families (HNC = 2; TE-HNC = 2) who dropped out of the study... Given the pilot nature of the project, we considered only complete data (i.e., data from participants available at pre- and post-treatment)." Comment: For the analysed child behavior outcomes, the report states that 19 families were included in the current analyses set at randomization (HNC n = 10; TE-HNC n = 9), but outcome analyses were restricted to completers only (Table 2: TE-HNC n = 7; HNC n = 8), indicating post-randomization loss of 2/10 (20%) in HNC and 2/9 (22%) in TE-HNC by post-treatment. Although losses were balanced and reasons were reported as major health/family stressors, the analysis used complete cases only rather than including all randomized participants, so the analysed outcome departs from random assignment and missing outcome data could bias results.
